sentences.text: Two young-- a young boy and a girl.
At 18, he had to join the army.
His name was-- I don't remember now.
And my mother's name was-- I've forgotten already.
We born-- born-- we were brought up bilingual.
I had five brothers and one sister.
Well, I was only a boy, a youngster in London.
I went to Jews' Free school-- at the old Jews' Free School, which is a well-known school in [INAUDIBLE].
It was a grade school and a central school.
Call it a high school, if you want to.
My two oldest brothers were in the printing trade.
And this-- the fourth one, the fourth brother I had, he was a taxi driver.
Plenty of cousins, but grandparents I only knew one, my grandmother.
She lived with her daughter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also in London.
And eventually when she was older, she went into the Jewish Home in London.
It is well known, that home.
My-- in London, number 8 Ruth House, Flower and Dean street.
Yes, in the heart of the East End.
Jews' Free School was a very well-known school.
There was about a thousand children then, because at the time children came from Russia from the pogroms.
And they all went to the Jewish school.
It was free school, because they didn't have to pay.
And they came poor-- they-- they came from Russia, poor.
The school provided them with clothes.
The boys all had suits given to them.
They were brown corduroy, and they had boots, heavy boots that should last long and corduroy suits.
And when you saw a boy wear a corduroy suit, you knew he was from Jews' Free School.
A Jewish school, we had to go to school on Sunday for two hours for Hebrew only.
It was a high school I went to.
I always wanted to be an engineer-- engineering.
When I left school, there was no work at all, not even for a boy.
It was a real depression then, and I was out of work ages until the people who lived opposite of me, they told my mother that their son's going to start a business on his own.
He was in the fur trade, and they're willing to take me in to learn the trade thoroughly.
I won't get much-- much pay, but I'll know the trade.
In the fur trade, I'll be a fur cutter, which was a high class job.
And that's where I finished up.
I was only seven when it started, and I remember the air raids when the Zeppelins came over.
And my mother was very excitable-- excitable.
And as soon as we heard the planes coming over-- airplane-- the airships coming over, the Zeppelins, she took me by the-- the hand, and we rushed to shelter.
I was only a little boy.
One got killed in France about a week before the armistice before the war finished in 1918.
And we all had to sit Shiva.
Well, I was a fur cutter.
I was trained, and I was an expert cutter.
And after a time, my employers decided they don't want the factory anymore.
We gave-- they're going to give it up.
They offered me to take over with one of the brothers-- his brothers, not my brothers-- and we became partners.
No, in the city of London.
That's where the fur trade was.
I had no time for that.
I had to go out and earn a living.
But, see, father had to work and--
There was no time for that.
In the early days of my youth, we used to go locally.
Like Brighton-- I'll mention the names, but most people wouldn't know it.
In Brighton or South End or so on.
When I grew older, we decided to go overseas.
So we went to Jersey, the Channel Islands.
She was on holiday [? there. ?] Then the next year, we went to-- further [? afield-- ?]
And while you were at school, did you ever learn to speak other languages?
Did you learn French or German or--
We started to learn German, but I left school by then.
We heard about it, but we didn't know much about it.
Because the people opposite, a relation of theirs came from Germany.
He left-- he fled, to put it another way.
And they told us so and so was a dentist in Germany, and he had to leave.
Well, just the fella on his own.
And you said you met your wife in Jersey?
When-- when did you meet her, and when did you get married?
We got engaged 1932, then two years later, we got married.
I don't know if you know it, Philpot Street.
People who lived in the East End would know it.
We went to a local one.
No, that was the big one.
They both died-- well, I got married in March, March the 18th.
My mother died in January of that year.
Practically together, about five or six weeks difference.
And then I had to get married, and I wanted to cancel the wedding.
So we went to the shul.
I went to speak to the rabbi.
A wedding, you don't-- you don't cancel.
So I got married without them.
My mother had a weak heart.
My father had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cancer.
Worked until the war broke out in 1939.
When-- that's going back into history now.
He was about two years when the war started.
Just before the war started, we had an inkling, a good idea that war's gonna breakout.
And I said to my wife, I've got to do something.
I'll join the civil defense, like locally.
If it's going to be air raids, well, I'll help.
I went to a local town hall and enlisted.
There was no vacancies normally, except in the casualty service.
I said, I'll do casualty work.
Started on first aid and war broke out.
Then we were called up in the civil defense.
How long I did it for?
For two years until the air raids eased up a bit, and we had to join the forces.
'41. '42, when the air raids eased up.
When the war started, the fur trade was finished.
Everybody fled or left the fur trade.
It was a luxury trade, so I was in that.
And who was in charge of that civil defense?
Do you remember who-- who was in charge there?
All we wore was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, wore a hat.
Yes, we wore a steel helmet.
And-- and were there a lot of casualties in that time?
In those first two years when you were a volunteer.
There was a lot of casualties, but actually, we weren't in the heart of the casualty area.
Mostly it was in the East End, which was full of people.
But we lived on the outskirts, so we didn't have many casualties.
1942 came along, and the captain said, all you young blokes go and join the forces.
I registered in the forces, and they said to me, what work you doing now?
I said, at the moment, army casualty service, which was true.
When I got my calling up papers, Royal Army Medical Corps.
Oh, it was immaterial, you know?
We went to training, three months' training [INAUDIBLE] exercises, drill and so on.
And then we were allocated a unit, which was gonna be our unit.
I was allocated to the field ambulance, because I was A1.
If you're not A1 in the army in the medical service, they put you in a hospital-- hospital work.
I was in a field ambulance.
That is with the infantry, because I was trained and A1.
They have the rank of an officer.
Though they're [? only ?] young-- they just got their medical degree-- soon as they became of the medical service, they had a rank officer.
We'd done all the spade work, but I was-- I was already trained.
You would have to go for exams in the army.
See, your classification in nursing service.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I had no classification, [? by ?] the first exam I had was NO3, nursing orderly, class three.
As you go through the army, you take more exams.
Then NO1, which is the highest.
That's the highest degree in the nursing service in the army.
You're trained as a medical nursing,
And you passed the exams and you got--
Till the end of the war.
And where did you do the training?
Did they-- did you stay in London or did they--
A place in Kent called Sittingbourne, Kent, near Chatham.
And there we were shifted up to Scotland.
We did all our training up in Scotland, because it's rough there.
Hills, lakes, and so on, because we were in the casualty service with the infantry.
We had to do the same work, same training as infantry, but we weren't allowed to hold a gun.
We were protected by the Geneva Convention.
We were never allowed to hold a gun, any rifle.
So we did the same training, but without arms.
And whereabouts in Scotland were you?
From the south up to Edinburgh, Glasgow, Inverness, right up north.
Then when the-- when the invasion was there, we were shifted over to the west coast.
Argyllshire, which is rugged and not many people there.
Civilians weren't allowed [? for ?] that part, and there we had to do our training, invasion training.
Well, going through the water fully clothed.
Doing training in water, climbing up mountains, which is-- Scotland has.
And we were shifted to the west coast, which is quiet [INAUDIBLE].
And there we were there until the invasion started.
All the [? thoughts ?] were good, except one.
A newcomer came along and he-- they soon shifted him out.
But all the others weren't anti-Semitic.
Did they give you any time for the Jewish holidays, or did they make provision for kosher food, anything like that?
Kosher food-- I had kosher food sent to me from London.
I registered as I don't eat non-kosher, and this organization in London then they sent [INAUDIBLE] and tin stuff from London.
What about the holidays and the Shabbat?
We had it at the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish boys got together in a different unit, and they had-- well, a make-believe service, yes.
Yes, but not for us only, for the area.
Later on became the Rabbi Harding.
And he conducted the services and--
The incident where somebody who was anti-Semitic in the--
And he was speaking about a Jew boy.
I didn't hear it, but we had a lot of Scots in our unit.
And when they heard about this, they set about him.
Don't you ever say that again.
See, I was a good-- a good [? turn to ?] them all.
And so how long were you in Scotland?
How long were you training there?
About five or six weeks then the invasion started, and we were shifted straight down to Portsmouth, the south-- south of England.
And there the landing craft was waiting-- waiting, and we were put on board, ready for invasion.
Actually, I was in the field unit-- we weren't wanted until a couple of days afterwards.
But we were at sea in the landing craft waiting to be called in.
And when we were wanted, then they brought us in.
On the beaches, land in the water.
And what happened when you when you came to Normandy?
Well, we landed on the beaches.
Not on the sand, in the water.
Waded through, got onto sand, and then we went onto land.
German planes were still about, and we had to go for cover.
We went into-- scampered to the nearest field, and we laid down by the hedge.
And we saw this plane coming over.
We thought it was one of ours.
We-- suddenly we saw him diving down towards us and machine gunning.
He drove along this hedge, machine gunning the hedge lengthwise.
Fortunately, the bullets landed about six inches away from me.
I could see in the soil the bullets going there.
It just bit wide for us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm trying to think [INAUDIBLE].
